title: Quick check: coordinating with Huntress auto-response

questions.prompt: Which action does auto-response take most commonly?

choices.label: Password reset.
choices.rationale: Sometimes, but session revoke is the most common auto-response action.

choices.label: Inbox-rule removal.
choices.rationale: Sometimes in certain configurations, but session revoke is the default.

choices.label: OAuth grant revocation.
choices.rationale: Not typically auto-response territory.

questions.explanation: Session revoke is the most common auto-response action. Some configurations also include password reset and rule removal.

questions.prompt: Which three things should you confirm about auto-response when reading the Incident Report?
choices: (1) What auto-response did; (2) when it happened relative to the attacker activity; (3) whether it completed cleanly.

choices.label: (1) Who authorised auto-response; (2) what the cost was; (3) who was on call.
choices.rationale: Not the named checks.

choices.label: (1) Whether the customer agreed; (2) whether the SOC re-reviewed; (3) whether the senior signed off.
choices.rationale: None of those are the named checks.

choices.label: (1) The user's seniority; (2) the tenant size; (3) the time of day.
choices.rationale: None of those are the named checks.

questions.explanation: What it did, when relative to attacker activity, and whether it completed cleanly. These three determine how you pick up the playbook.

questions.prompt: Auto-response did session revoke. What is your next move?

choices.label: Run the full playbook from step 1, including a re-revoke.
choices.rationale: Re-revoking wastes effort and creates duplicate audit entries.

choices: Skip step 1 (session revoke). Verify it applied cleanly. Proceed to step 2 (password and MFA reset).

choices.label: Wait for the SOC to update the Recommendation.
choices.rationale: Wrong delay. The Recommendation usually already accounts for auto-response.

choices.label: Close the incident. Auto-response handled everything.
choices.rationale: Auto-response usually did session revoke only. Remaining steps are still pending.

questions.explanation: Acknowledge the auto-response action. Verify it applied. Continue from where the platform left off.

questions.prompt: You are at the password reset step. Marcus calls angry: "Why am I locked out? Reverse it." What do you do?

choices.label: Reverse the auto-response session revoke to restore his access.
choices.rationale: Auto-response is the containment. Reversing puts Marcus back in the compromised state.

choices: Stand your ground: explain the compromise, the platform's auto-action, and the rest of the response. Have the password handoff ready as the next step.

choices.label: Tell Marcus the SOC made the call and you cannot reverse it.
choices.rationale: Passes the buck. Own the action and explain the trade-off.

questions.explanation: The action stands. Marcus hears the why, the timeline, and a forward-looking path. Owning the action builds trust.

questions.prompt: "Re-doing actions auto-response already completed" has what consequence?
choices: Wastes effort, can confuse the audit trail, and in some edge cases creates duplicate-action states the SOC has to untangle.

choices.label: There is no consequence. Re-running is safe.
choices.rationale: The lesson names specific costs.

choices.label: It triggers an automatic billing event.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: It causes the auto-response system to disable itself.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: Three costs: wasted effort, confused audit trail, potential duplicate-action states.

questions.prompt: Skipping verification is acceptable when auto-response says the action worked.
questions.explanation: Same pattern as the EDR verify-step skip. Most of the time auto-response is fine. The times it is not are the ones that bite you later. Trust-but-verify.

questions.prompt: A user calls to complain about the lockout. The tech restores the session without checking the Incident Report. What is the failure?
choices: Fighting auto-response. The auto-action was the containment. Reversing it puts the customer back in the compromise state.

choices.label: Fine. Customer-service reflex.
choices.rationale: Customer complaint does not justify reversing a security containment action.

choices.label: The Incident Report does not matter when the customer is on the phone.
choices.rationale: Checking the Incident Report is the discipline.

choices.label: Auto-response actions can be undone freely.
choices.rationale: They should not be undone without senior involvement.

questions.explanation: The auto-response action was the containment. Reversing it without context restores the compromise state.

questions.prompt: Auto-response shows as failed or partial. The Recommendation does not direct manual completion. What do you do?

choices.label: Re-trigger auto-response yourself.
choices.rationale: The tech does not re-trigger platform auto-response.

choices.label: Wait 24 hours for auto-response to retry.
choices.rationale: Wrong delay. Bump is the action.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket.
choices.rationale: Senior is the first escalation path.

questions.explanation: Failed or partial auto-response without manual-completion guidance escalates to senior.

questions.prompt: Auto-response took an action that the Recommendation now contradicts. What is the right move?
choices: Surface the conflict to the SOC with the specifics. They own the disposition.

choices.label: Reverse auto-response's action to match the Recommendation.
choices.rationale: Reversing containment is the failure pattern. Surface to SOC instead.

choices.label: Ignore the Recommendation; auto-response is more authoritative.
choices.rationale: The Recommendation is current guidance. The conflict needs surfacing.

choices.label: Apply both actions and let the platform sort it out.
choices.rationale: Does not resolve the conflict.

questions.explanation: Conflict between auto-action and current Recommendation is rare. Surface the specifics to the SOC and let them disposition.

questions.prompt: Auto-response fired 6 minutes after the suspicious sign-in (instead of seconds). Why does the timing matter?
choices: Six minutes means the attacker had time to act before containment: create rules, grant OAuth, tamper with MFA. The remaining manual steps may address more artefacts than in a fast-response scenario.

choices.label: It does not matter. Six seconds or six minutes, the response is the same.
choices.rationale: The attacker's window of activity before containment determines what artefacts the playbook addresses.

choices.label: Six minutes means auto-response failed.
choices.rationale: It completed, just slowly. Slow is not failed.

choices.label: Six minutes means the SOC was delayed.
choices.rationale: Auto-response timing is platform-driven, not SOC-driven.

questions.explanation: The gap between attacker activity start and auto-response determines how much the attacker accomplished before containment. More time means more artefacts to clean up.
